[^0]: *The first two authors contributed equally. --- Round 2 --- I've opened the References section (DOC1_A10) and examined all the references cited by the TinyLlama paper. I need to check if any of these references are the other provided papers: - DOC2: "Lory: Fully Differentiable Mixture-of-Experts for Autoregressive Language Model Pre-training" - DOC3: "OpenMoE: An Early Effort on Open Mixture-of-Experts Language Models" Looking through the reference list, I don't see either "Lory" or "OpenMoE" mentioned. The references include papers like: - Llama (Touvron et al.) - Pythia (Biderman et al.) - OPT (Zhang et al.) - FlashAttention (Dao) - SlimPajama (Soboleva et al.) - Starcoder (Li et al.) - Various benchmark papers (MMLU, Hellaswag, etc.) None of the provided papers (DOC2: Lory or DOC3: OpenMoE) appear in this reference list. This means DOC1 (TinyLlama) does NOT reference DOC2 or DOC3. Now I need to check if there's any mention of these papers in other sections of DOC1, like the Introduction, to be thorough. Let me also check the Introduction section to see if there's any related work discussion that might mention these MoE papers. But first, let me also verify by checking the Introduction section to see if there are any mentions of MoE (Mixture-of-Experts) models that could relate to DOC2 or DOC3. {"action":"open","anchor_id":"DOC1_A2","expected_evidence":"Introduction section to check if TinyLlama mentions or discusses Lory or OpenMoE papers in related work or introduction"} Recent progress in natural language processing (NLP) has been largely propelled by scaling up language model sizes (Brown et al., 2020; Chowdhery et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023a,b). Large Language Models (LLMs) pre-trained on extensive text corpora have demonstrated their effectiveness on a wide range of tasks (OpenAI, 2023; Touvron et al., 2023b). Some empirical studies demonstrated emergent abilities in LLMs, abilities that may only manifest in models with a sufficiently large number of parameters, such as few-shot prompting (Brown et al., 2020) and chain-of-thought reasoning (Wei et al., 2022). Other studies focus on modeling the scaling behavior of LLMs (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). Hoffmann et al. (2022) suggest that, to train a compute-optimal model, the size of the model and the amount of training data should be increased at the same rate. This provides a guideline on how to optimally select the model size and allocate the amount of training data when the compute budget is fixed.[^0] Although these works show a clear preference on large models, the potential of training smaller models with larger dataset remains under-explored. Instead of training compute-optimal language models, Touvron et al. (2023a) highlight the importance of the inference budget, instead of focusing solely on training compute-optimal language models. Inference-optimal language models aim for optimal performance within specific inference constraints This is achieved by training models with more tokens than what is recommended by the scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022). Touvron et al. (2023a) demonstrates that smaller models, when trained with more data, can match or even outperform their larger counterparts. Also, Thaddé (2023) suggest that existing scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) may not predict accurately in situations where smaller models are trained for longer periods. Motivated by these new findings, this work focuses on exploring the behavior of smaller models when trained with a significantly larger number of tokens than what is suggested by the scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022). Specifically, we train a Transformer decoder-only model (Vaswani et al., 2017) with 1.1B parameters using approximately 3 trillion tokens. To our knowledge, this is the first attempt to train a model with 1B parameters using such a large amount of data. Following the same architecture and tokenizer as Llama 2 (Touvron et al., 2023b), we name our model TinyLlama. TinyLlama shows competitive performance compared to existing open-source language models of similar sizes. Specifically, TinyLlama surpasses both OPT-1.3B (Zhang et al., 2022) and Pythia1.4B (Biderman et al., 2023) in various downstream tasks. Our TinyLlama is open-source, aimed at improving accessibility for researchers in language model research. We believe its excellent performance and compact size make it an attractive platform for researchers and practitioners in language model research. ## 2 Pretraining --- Round 3 --- I've now examined both the References section (DOC1_A10) and the Introduction section (DOC1_A2) of the TinyLlama paper (DOC1). From my analysis: 1. **References (what DOC1 cites)**: The References section lists many papers including Llama, Pythia, OPT, FlashAttention, SlimPajama, Starcoder, and various benchmark papers. I did NOT find "Lory: Fully Differentiable Mixture-of-Experts for Autoregressive Language Model Pre-training" (DOC2) or "OpenMoE: An Early Effort on Open Mixture-of-Experts Language Models" (DOC3) in the reference list. 2. **Introduction (DOC1_A2)**: This section discusses scaling laws, LLMs, and mentions papers like Brown et al. (2020), Chowdhery et al. (2022), Touvron et al. (2023a,b), Hoffmann et al. (2022), etc. There is no mention of Lory or OpenMoE papers here either. The Introduction focuses on: - Scaling model sizes (Brown et al., 2020; Chowdhery et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023a,b) - Inference-optimal language models (Touvron et al., 2023a) - Training smaller models with larger datasets - TinyLlama being the first 1B parameter model trained with ~3 trillion tokens There's no mention of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models like Lory or OpenMoE in this introduction. Since DOC1 (TinyLlama) does not appear to reference DOC2 (Lory) or DOC3 (OpenMoE), the Reference list for DOC1 should be empty. For Citations (who cites DOC1), I would need to check DOC2 and DOC3 to see if they cite TinyLlama. Let me look at the Abstract (DOC1_A1) to be thorough. {"action":"open","anchor_id":"DOC1_A1","expected_evidence":"Abstract section to check for any mention of Lory or OpenMoE papers"} # TinyLlama: An Open-Source Small Language Model Peiyuan Zhang* Guangtao Zeng* Tianduo Wang Wei Lu
StatNLP Research Group
Singapore University of Technology and Design
\{peiyuan_zhang, tianduo_wang, luwei\}@sutd.edu.sg
guangtao_zeng@mymail.sutd.edu.sg ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2024_06_04_cb33f1cfea6550840308g-1.jpg?height=539&width=615&top_left_y=842&top_left_x=733) #### Abstract We present TinyLlama, a compact 1.1B language model pretrained on around 1 trillion tokens for approximately 3 epochs. Building on the architecture and tokenizer of Llama 2 (Touvron et al., 2023b), TinyLlama leverages various advances contributed by the open-source community (e.g., FlashAttention (Dao, 2023)), achieving better computational efficiency. Despite its relatively small size, TinyLlama demonstrates remarkable performance in a series of downstream tasks. It significantly outperforms existing open-source language models with comparable sizes. Our model checkpoints and code are publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/jzhang38/TinyLlama. ## 1 Introduction --- Round 4 --- I've now examined the Abstract (DOC1_A1), References (DOC1_A10), and Introduction (DOC1_A2) sections of DOC1 (TinyLlama). Based on this evidence, DOC1 (TinyLlama) does NOT reference DOC2 (Lory) or DOC3 (OpenMoE).
